retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Before rushing to decompression of the pericardium, the patient should be provided with oxygen, volume expansion and bed rest with legs elevated. If possible, positive pressure mechanical ventilation should be avoided as it may further decrease venous return and aggravate the symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Barotrauma and Mechanical Ventilation(Archived) -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumomediastinum is an exception. In tension pneumomediastinum, the pressure in the mediastinum increases significantly and ultimately may lead to physiological effects similar to pericardial tamponade. It may cause compression of the great vessels and compromise the right heart filling leading to shock and eventually, cardiac arrest. Chest radiography will show a heart silhouette resembling a sphere. The initial management involves ventilator changes to decrease the airway pressures and increase the FiO2 in the ventilator to 100%. Ultimately surgical mediastinal decompression is required. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2362
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular cannula insertion is a broad procedural category that includes the placement of a pulmonary artery catheter (قثطرة الشريان الرئوي) as one of its specific techniques. Pulmonary artery catheterization involves advancing a specialized cannula through a central vein into the pulmonary artery to obtain direct hemodynamic measurements such as pulmonary artery pressure, cardiac output, and mixed venous oxygen saturation. This subprocedure is commonly employed in critical care and perioperative settings to guide fluid management, assess cardiac function, and monitor patients with complex cardiopulmonary conditions.

It comprises systemic arterial pressure monitoring, continuous electrocardiogram monitoring (including 10‑hour portrait, fetal ECG, ECG‑pressure monitoring during major surgery, and combined ECG‑pressure‑blood‑gas‑cardiac‑output monitoring), and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring. Hemodynamic monitoring is a subtype of “procedures on blood vessels” and is conceptually linked to Swan‑Ganz catheterization for advanced pulmonary‑artery pressure assessment. Catheter‑based therapeutic procedures form a related family that includes catheter ablation of cardiac tissue, coronary vascular interventions (“رأب وعائي تاجي”), pulmonary‑artery catheterization (“قثطرة الشريان الرئوي”), and irrigation of vascular catheters; all share principles of intravascular access, device manipulation, and physiological monitoring. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 931
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulation with cardiac‑output measurement is classified as a form of other vascular catheterization that does not involve direct instrumentation of the heart and is performed by percutaneously inserting an arterial catheter for continuous hemodynamic monitoring. The procedure can be carried out in a wide range of arterial sites—including the left and right axillary arteries, left and right carotid arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, and left and right temporal arteries—providing routes for real‑time assessment of arterial pressure, flow, and cardiac performance. Within the broader domain of cardiovascular monitoring, clinicians integrate data from electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring, arterial pressure recordings, blood‑gas analysis, and cardiac‑output measurements, and they may also employ transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring via an esophageal probe to obtain detailed ultrasound images of the heart and great vessels. Together, these invasive arterial catheterizations and imaging techniques enable comprehensive, continuous surveillance of circulatory function, guiding therapeutic decisions, enhancing patient safety, and improving outcomes in peri‑operative and critical‑care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamics. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output. In addition to these non‑invasive and semi‑invasive approaches, cardiovascular monitoring places particular emphasis on the percutaneous insertion of catheters for arterial pressure monitoring, targeting the left axillary artery, left femoral artery, left radial artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. These arterial catheterizations provide continuous waveform and pressure data essential for intra‑operative and critical‑care management. Furthermore, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, offering real‑time imaging of cardiac structures and function. Together, these interconnected methods form an integrated strategy for detailed cardiovascular assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of specialized techniques used to assess cardiac function and related physiological parameters in various clinical settings. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also extends to the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous measurement, with particular attention to catheter placement in the left carotid artery, left femoral artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a form of cardiovascular monitoring, providing detailed imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus. Together, these interconnected methods enable precise real‑time evaluation of cardiac and hemodynamic status, supporting informed decision‑making and optimal patient care during surgical procedures, fetal assessment, and critical care management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 903
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques aimed at assessing cardiac function and systemic hemodynamics. It includes electrocardiogram monitoring such as the “Electrocardiogram monitoring, 10 hour portrait” and specialized fetal electrocardiogram, as well as comprehensive monitoring of ECG, arterial pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. Invasive arterial monitoring is achieved through percutaneous insertion of catheters into various vessels, including the left carotid artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, right femoral artery, and right temporal artery, each providing direct pressure and flow data from the respective arterial sites. Cardiovascular monitoring also incorporates transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, which offers real‑time imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus, and hemodynamické monitorování (hemodynamic monitoring), which evaluates overall circulatory status. Together, these modalities provide clinicians with detailed information for the diagnosis, management, and therapeutic guidance of patients with cardiovascular and systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2378
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamic status, particularly in the peri‑operative setting. It includes specific child procedures such as Monitoring of ECG at surgery and Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, which together provide continuous electrical, pressure, respiratory gas, and flow information. A central focus of cardiovascular monitoring is the percutaneous insertion of catheters into arterial sites for direct pressure and waveform acquisition; these sites include the left femoral artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, and right radial artery. Each arterial catheterization allows real‑time assessment of arterial pressure and can be integrated with other monitoring modalities. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, offering ultrasound‑based visualization of cardiac structures and function from the esophagus. Together, these interconnected monitoring strategies enable comprehensive evaluation of cardiovascular status, guide intra‑operative decision‑making, and support optimal patient care.

The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also extends to the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for real‑time data acquisition, with specific techniques described for catheter placement in the left femoral artery, left radial artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, and right temporal artery. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: Other vascular catheterization — not involving the heart — is defined as a broad procedural class that includes all percutaneous and open catheter‑based interventions on arteries or veins without direct cardiac manipulation. It is a subtype of “procedure on artery” and serves as the parent category for numerous monitoring, therapeutic and interventional techniques. Monitoring catheters under this class comprise arterial cannulation for cardiac‑output measurement and continuous hemodynamic surveillance. Reported arterial sites include left and right axillary, carotid, femoral, radial, temporal and left radial arteries. These catheters provide systemic arterial pressure monitoring, ECG, blood‑gas analysis and cardiac‑output data during surgery and intensive‑care care. Cardiovascular monitoring also incorporates transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, general hemodynamic monitoring and Swan‑Ganz (pulmonary‑artery flotation) catheterization, the latter being a foundational parent

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of specialized procedures aimed at continuously assessing cardiac function and related physiological parameters. It includes various electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring techniques such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output. In addition to these ECG‑based modalities, cardiovascular monitoring places particular emphasis on the percutaneous insertion of catheters into specific arterial sites for invasive hemodynamic assessment; the targeted arteries include the left radial artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, right femoral artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is also classified as a form of cardiovascular monitoring, reflecting its role in providing detailed, real‑time imaging of cardiac structures and function. Together, these interconnected monitoring strategies enable precise evaluation of cardiovascular status during routine care, surgical procedures, and fetal assessment, thereby supporting informed clinical decision‑making and optimal patient outcomes.
